title: doesn't seem possible to have a signed image with multiple tags in a registry that requires signature lookaside
body:
/kind bug


**Description**

Perhaps a question, not a bug, but I can't figure out...  How can we sign an image with 2 different tags when using a registry that requires signature lookaside store?

In our CICD workflow, we build a docker image and first push it with a timestamp tag.  If the image passes various quality checks, we add the tag "latest".  I've recently started signing our images and all goes as expected when I use a registry that natively supports Pod Manager signatures.  But if I use a registry that doesn't support Pod Manager signatures (artifactory), everything works fine with the initial timestamp tag.  When I skopeo or podman-push using sign-by, I get the signature file in my local staging folder and I can curl it up to the lookaside repo (an artifactory generic repo) and with proper policy.json blob, I can pull and have the signature matched back up and verified.  The problem comes when I try to apply the `latest` tag and push that and it's signature.  What happens is, a new (different) signature gets written to the the signature staging area but it has the same filename (image-name@sha256=...../signature-1) as the first signature.  If I curl that up to the lookaside repo, I can no longer pull the original image -- pulling `latest` now works.

It seems, based on the signature looksaside file naming, it's only possible to have a signed image with 1 tag in a registry that requires signature lookaside  --  imageid@sha256=  path is the same regardless of tag but signature itself changes based on tag...  so you can't have 2 signatures in lookaside repo for same image based on sha256 filename

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. podman build -t test:1234
2. skopeo copy --dest-creds ****:**** --sign-by <key> containers-storage:test:1234 docker://registry/test:**1234**
3. observe signature file in staging -- note filename and size, perhaps checksum it -- rename staging directory for later comparison
```
+ find /tmp/sigstore -ls
 49686832      4 drwxr-xr-x   3  jenkins  jenkins      4096 Mar  3 19:13 /tmp/sigstore
 49686845      4 drwxr-xr-x   2  jenkins  jenkins      4096 Mar  3 19:13 /tmp/sigstore/test@sha256=c71f714331fefbc11dc3ea6b2790e49b07dbc6d1187f373206e3d08022410ef1
 49686846      4 -rw-r--r--   1  jenkins  jenkins       654 Mar  3 19:13 /tmp/sigstore/test@sha256=c71f714331fefbc11dc3ea6b2790e49b07dbc6d1187f373206e3d08022410ef1/signature-1
```
4. skopeo copy --dest-creds ****:**** --sign-by <key> containers-storage:test:1234 docker://registry/test:**latest**
5. observe file with same name and different contents has been written to staging -- diff against saved staging from step 3 and not the contents are different
```
+ find /tmp/sigstore -ls
 49686832      4 drwxr-xr-x   3  jenkins  jenkins      4096 Mar  3 19:13 /tmp/sigstore
 49686845      4 drwxr-xr-x   2  jenkins  jenkins      4096 Mar  3 19:13 /tmp/sigstore/test@sha256=c71f714331fefbc11dc3ea6b2790e49b07dbc6d1187f373206e3d08022410ef1
 49686846      4 -rw-r--r--   1  jenkins  jenkins       644 Mar  3 19:13 /tmp/sigstore/test@sha256=c71f714331fefbc11dc3ea6b2790e49b07dbc6d1187f373206e3d08022410ef1/signature-1
```

**Describe the results you received:**
got a new signature file with same name that's different than the signature for the first tag with different contents which will verify the :latest tagged image but not the :1234 tagged image -- no way to store the file in the lookaside repo to distunguish it from the first signature file/structure

**Describe the results you expected:**
expected either a signature that was the same as the first, or a signature file/structure with name(s) that differed from the first so it could be stored in lookaside repo distinct from original signature

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**
pushing/pulling the same image with different tags works fine with a repo that natively supports Pod Manager signatures
same result using podman tag sequence as skopeo -- skopeo just makes clearer example

**Output of `podman version`:**

```
podman version 2.2.1
```

**Output of `podman info --debug`:**

```

@vrothberg Any thoughts here, or is this a Miloslav issue?

> @vrothberg Any thoughts here, or is this a Miloslav issue?

That exceeds my (basic) understanding of the signing code.
Assigning to @mtrmac to have a look when time allows.

Drive-by first look:

See `putSignaturesToLookaside`:
- The conceptually consistent way is to replace
  > `skopeo copy --sign-by … containers-storage:test:1234 docker://registry/test:latest`

  with 
  > `skopeo copy --sign-by … docker://registry/test:1234 docker://registry/test:latest`

  so that the original signature is visible to the copy process. That has a major downside in that this is only secure if the registry is trusted, the source reference uses a digest, or or the `test:1234` read enforces a valid signature.
- It might make sense to modify the code to add new files instead of overwriting; the X-R-S-S implementation does that, for comparison. I’m not 100% sure, but leaning towards “yes” – as this shows, the staging directory is shared for all “images” with the same digest, not unique to a single `dockerImageReference` / image as an “atomic unit”.

That did the trick for my use case @mtrmac -- thank you!  In our case, we do trust the registry so I can use the pull and push back skopeo (docker to docker) to add the second tag.  Doing it that way generates the signature-2 file and once I store both signature-1 and signature-2, verifying the image with either tag works.

This solution works for me so OK to close this issue. 

```

(Also, if you are using manual `curl` calls to upload signatures, and if you know for sure that there will be exactly those two, it would be perfectly safe to upload the created `signature-1` to the registry as a `signature-2`; the internal contents are index-independent. I appreciate that that’s not helpful in the general case, with a pre-existing image with an unknown number of signatures.)

This is actually all being done in Jenkins pipelines...   examples above were by hand for reproducibility.  So, first I push and get signature-1, curl that up so the second skopeo will be able to verify the signature, skopeo docker-to-docker as you recommended, which gets me signature 2, then curl up all the signatures (yes, results in re-sending singature-1 but this way will handle "all the signatures" in case there are more at some point...)  Thanks again -- working great!

```

	sh """
		skopeo copy --src-creds ${DOCKER_USERNAME}:${DOCKER_PASSWORD} --dest-creds ${DOCKER_USERNAME}:${DOCKER_PASSWORD} --sign-by ${podmanSigningFpr} docker://${imageName} docker://${imageLatest}
	"""
	if (podmanSigStore != null) {
		sh """#!/bin/bash -x
			for sig in \$(cd ${podmanSigStoreStaging};find * -type f); do
				curl -u ${DOCKER_USERNAME}:${DOCKER_PASSWORD} -X PUT "https://${podmanSigStore}/\$sig" -T "${podmanSigStoreStaging}/\$sig"
			done
		"""
	}
```
